text: Hiatal hernia repair on the da Vinci XI system.
The surgeon begins the hiatal hernia repair by locating the pars flaccida of the gastro-hepatic ligament.
The surgeon uses the vessel sealer extend to incise the gastro-hepatic ligament and continues the dissection upwards towards the right crura.
The surgeon approaches the right crural muscle, exchanging the assistant's grip from the pars flaccida to the fat tissue directly adjacent to the crural muscle.
The surgeon exposes the peritoneal covering on the right cruce during the hiatal hernia repair, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum over the curl muscle to access the mediastinum, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon takes small bites with the instrument, gradually exposing the muscle fibers beneath the tissue.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal dissection along the entire length of the right cruce, with the curl fibers clearly visible.
The assistant uses their hand to grasp the edge of the hernia sac, aiding in retraction while the surgeon bluntly spreads tissue within the mediastinum.
The surgeon identifies the presence of areolar tissue, indicating entry into the correct avascular plane during the dissection process.
The surgeon continues blunt dissection within the avascular plane to mobilize the esophagus and hernia contents, maintaining careful separation of tissue layers.
With good retraction and visualization of the right mediastinum, the surgeon continues dissection in the avascular plane to mobilize the esophagus on the right side.
The posterior vagus nerve becomes visible, lying within the fatty tissue on the right side of the mediastinum.
We will be careful to preserve the nerve during dissection.
The surgeon focuses on the left cruce, carefully dissecting and removing the phrenogastric fat pad to expose underlying structures.
The surgeon removes the phrenogastric fat pad to expose the edge of the left crural muscle, enhancing visibility for further dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the left crux, paying close attention to the proximity of the anterior vagus nerve, which is often closely approximated to this anatomical structure just proximal to the apex.
With the curl muscle exposed, the surgeon gently spreads within the left mediastinum, utilizing the areolar planes to separate tissue layers.
The surgeon gently spreads the anatomical structures away from the apex of the left crux, carefully dissecting the tissue to avoid injury to the esophagus and anterior vagus nerve.
The surgeon carefully dissects the tissue anterior to the esophagus, revealing the anterior vagus nerve lying within the tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects the tissue near the left crux, ensuring preservation of the anterior vagus nerve, which is closely approximated to the surgical area.
The surgeon retracts the curl muscle with their left hand during the dissection, maintaining exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon optimizes visualization for blunt dissection using their right hand, focusing on the exposed curl muscle and surrounding structures within the left mediastinum.
The surgeon begins the posterior dissection, carefully manipulating the tissue behind the esophagus while preserving the anatomical integrity of surrounding structures.
The surgeon begins the dissection by identifying the posterior confluence of the cruse, focusing on the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon gently spreads over the left muscle fibers from the right side, using the left hand to push mediastinal structures upward while ligating posterior attachments with the right hand.
The surgeon has created a clear retroesophageal window, revealing the anatomical space behind the esophagus for further dissection.
The surgeon passes a Penrose drain around the esophagus to encircle it, providing retraction for improved access and visibility during the remainder of the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon refines the posterior dissection area, ensuring clear separation of tissues before proceeding to the dissection on the left side.
The surgeon takes down the remaining mediastinal attachments on the left side to complete the mobilization of the esophagus, ensuring clear separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon has achieved two to three centimeters of intra-abdominal length of the esophagus and prepares for the closure of the diaphragmatic crura.
The surgeon begins the closure by using their left hand to protect the aorta while driving a needle through the cruce with their right hand.
The surgeon pulls the suture through while adjusting the camera position to allow for larger movements within the abdominal cavity.
This will make me more efficient during suturing.
The surgeon brings the camera closer to the cruce to continue sewing, providing a detailed view of the needle and suture placement in the surgical field.
The surgeon stabilizes the suture loop on the curl muscle, preparing to take the next bite with the needle during the same movement.
The surgeon closes the hiatus with minimal tension, ensuring the anatomical structures are approximated without excessive force.

However, when closing defects with wide or curl separation, you may need to lay in several sutures before sequentially pulling them through and taking off the tension, much like you would in a fascial closure of a ventral hernia.
The surgeon continues running the suture along the cura to approximate the diaphragm around the esophagus, ensuring circumferential closure without compressing the esophagus.
The surgeon tightens the last suture bite, releasing tension on the esophagus to allow it to fill the entire space of the hiatus without compression.
The surgeon locks the suture by running it back on itself two to three times, securing the closure of the hiatus with visible loops of suture material.
The surgeon completes the hiatal hernia repair, ensuring the diaphragm approximates the esophagus circumferentially without compression, preparing for the subsequent LES augmentation.
